content:
First observe that for a positive integer \(n\), its digit‐product \(P(n)\ge0\).  The equation is

 P(n) = n^2 − 15n − 27.

The quadratic \(n^2−15n−27\) is negative for \(1\le n\le16\) and becomes nonnegative only for \(n\ge17\).  On the other hand, if \(n\) has
- 1 digit then \(P(n)\le9\), while \(n^2−15n−27<0\) for \(n\le16\),
- 2 digits then \(P(n)\le9\cdot9=81\), but \(n^2−15n−27>81\) already as soon as \(n\ge21\).

Hence any solution must lie in \(17\le n\le20\).  Checking those four:

\(n=17:\quad P(17)=1\cdot7=7,\quad 17^2-15\cdot17-27=289-255-27=7\)\quad ⇒ works.  
\(n=18:\;P=8,\;18^2-15\cdot18-27=27\) (no)  
\(n=19:\;P=9,\;19^2-15\cdot19-27=49\) (no)  
\(n=20:\;P=0,\;20^2-15\cdot20-27=73\) (no)

The only solution is \(n=17\), so the sum of all solutions is

\(\boxed{17}\).

 P(n) = n^2 − 15n − 27.

The quadratic \(n^2−15n−27\) is negative for \(1\le n\le16\) and becomes nonnegative only for \(n\ge17\).  On the other hand, if \(n\) has
- 1 digit then \(P(n)\le9\), while \(n^2−15n−27<0\) for \(n\le16\),
- 2 digits then \(P(n)\le9\cdot9=81\), but \(n^2−15n−27>81\) already as soon as \(n\ge21\).

Hence any solution must lie in \(17\le n\le20\).  Checking those four:

\(n=17:\quad P(17)=1\cdot7=7,\quad 17^2-15\cdot17-27=289-255-27=7\)\quad ⇒ works.  
\(n=18:\;P=8,\;18^2-15\cdot18-27=27\) (no)  
\(n=19:\;P=9,\;19^2-15\cdot19-27=49\) (no)  
\(n=20:\;P=0,\;20^2-15\cdot20-27=73\) (no)

\(\boxed{17}\).
